purpose: Invite-link record model, expiry/usage helpers, and one-shot idempotent migration. Extends the existing idb-keyval-backed InviteLink store with a real lifecycle: expiresAt, usageCount, and the expiryNotified/expiryAcknowledged notification flags. Owns the single expiry predicate (isExpired) that every other module (joinFlow's gate, manageOverlay's UI, notifications' sweep) must consult instead of recomputing expiry inline.

public_api.name: DAY_MS
public_api.description: 86_400_000 — one day in milliseconds. Single source of truth for the fixed expiry window; no other literal 86400000 exists in the module.

public_api.name: InviteLink
public_api.signature: export interface InviteLink { nonce: string; groupId: string; createdAt: number; expiresAt: number; usageCount: number; expiryNotified: boolean; expiryAcknowledged: boolean; label?: string; muted: boolean }
public_api.description: Extended record shape. expiresAt/usageCount/expiryNotified/expiryAcknowledged are typed required (new code always writes all four at creation), but a record loaded from idb-keyval may in practice be a pre-existing legacy record missing them at runtime — every read site MUST go through isExpired (for expiry) and the ?? fallback pattern, never assume the type guarantee holds for data already on disk.

public_api.name: isExpired
public_api.description: now >= (link.expiresAt ?? link.createdAt + DAY_MS). Pure, stateless, no memoization — computes effectiveExpiry fresh on every call so it is correct against un-migrated legacy records read via a single getInviteLink (the join-request gate's read path), independent of whether migrateInviteLinks has run.

public_api.name: buildNewInviteLink
public_api.signature: buildNewInviteLink(params: { nonce: string; groupId: string; createdAt: number; label?: string }): InviteLink
public_api.description: Pure factory for a freshly-created record: expiresAt = createdAt + DAY_MS, usageCount = 0, expiryNotified = false, expiryAcknowledged = false, muted = false (AC-MODEL-2). Added so GenerateInviteLinkModal.tsx's diff is a single call site rather than an inlined createdAt + DAY_MS computation, and so creation-time defaults are unit-testable without jsdom.

public_api.name: incrementInviteLinkUsage
public_api.description: Load-modify-save: usageCount += 1, persisted. Silent no-op (never throws, never creates a record) when nonce does not resolve. Concurrent calls against the SAME nonce are serialized through a per-nonce promise chain (see Concurrency Strategy below) so two racing approvals cannot lose an increment.

public_api.name: markInviteLinkExpiryNotified
public_api.description: Sets expiryNotified = true and persists; leaves expiryAcknowledged/usageCount/expiresAt untouched. Silent no-op when nonce does not resolve. Serialized via the same per-nonce lock.

public_api.name: markInviteLinkExpiryAcknowledged
public_api.description: Sets expiryAcknowledged = true and persists; leaves expiryNotified/usageCount/expiresAt untouched. Silent no-op when nonce does not resolve. Serialized via the same per-nonce lock.

public_api.name: migrateInviteLinks
public_api.description: One-shot, idempotent, store-wide migration. For each record: if all four of expiresAt/usageCount/expiryNotified/expiryAcknowledged are already present AND the record is not muted, the record is a total no-op (not even read back out as changed) — this is what makes repeated startup calls, and calls against records the sweep has since touched, safe. Otherwise: backfills any missing field (expiresAt = createdAt + DAY_MS, usageCount = 0, flags = false), then for a non-muted record whose expiresAt was itself the field that was missing (a genuinely legacy record) and whose backfilled expiresAt <= now, stamps expiryNotified = true (retroactive-expiry notification suppression, Design Decision 3) — a record that already carried its own expiresAt is never re-stamped by this path, so migrateInviteLinks running again at a later app-startup never steals a notification the sweep is responsible for. For a muted record (regardless of missing fields), clamps expiresAt = min(effectiveExpiry, now) and stamps expiryNotified = true unconditionally (Design Decision 4); this clamp is a mathematical fixed point (min is idempotent under a non-decreasing now), so reprocessing an already-fully-migrated muted record on a later call is safe and produces byte-for-byte identical output, satisfying AC-MIGRATE-6 without a separate muted-specific skip branch. Concurrent invocations (StrictMode double-effect, overlapping startup calls) are deduplicated via a module-level in-flight promise latch; sequential invocations after the first perform zero writes (verified via a per-record equality check before calling idb-keyval's set).

public_api.name: createInviteLinkStore
public_api.description: Unchanged from pre-existing code — returns the idb-keyval store handle.

public_api.name: saveInviteLink
public_api.description: Unchanged signature; callers (GenerateInviteLinkModal, relayBackup restore) now must supply the four new fields since InviteLink types them as required.

public_api.name: getInviteLink
public_api.description: Unchanged. The join-request gate's sole read path (S2) — never passes through migrateInviteLinks, which is exactly why isExpired's own fallback must be load-bearing.

public_api.name: loadAllInviteLinks
public_api.description: Unchanged. Consumed by relayBackup and (in a later story) the sweep.

public_api.name: updateInviteLinkMuted
public_api.description: Unchanged behavior; now routed through the same per-nonce lock as the other single-record mutators for consistency. Still used by the gate/legacy UI until S3 removes the mute toggle.

concurrency_strategy.rationale: idb-keyval's get/set on a single key has an await gap between read and write; two concurrent calls against the same nonce would otherwise both read the same stale value and one increment would be lost. Chaining each call onto a per-nonce promise serializes the read-modify-write without a cross-tab/cross-worker lock (out of scope — this project has no SharedWorker/BroadcastChannel coordination layer, and Design Decision 3 confines all invite-link state to a single device/session anyway).
concurrency_strategy.migration_strategy: separate module-level single in-flight promise latch (not per-nonce) since migrateInviteLinks touches the whole store, not one record; concurrent calls dedupe onto the same in-flight promise, sequential calls after the first are no-ops by construction (see migrateInviteLinks description).
